parts.name: 186_attB

parts.name: 186_attP
parts.description: Bacteriophage 186 phage attachment site (attP) for the 186 tyrosine integrase. 307 bp spanning the full attP functional region: Int arm-type recognition sequences on the P (left) and P' (right) arms, a single P-arm IHF site, the tRNA-Ile/attB common core (BOB') with the predicted 7-bp staggered crossover, a right-arm IHF site, and five Apl (recombination directionality factor) operators. 186 is a P2-family lambdoid coliphage; integration into the host chromosome (within the ileY tRNA-Ile locus) requires 186 Int + IHF, while Apl directs excision. functional_claims.label: AviTag is site-specifically biotinylated on Lys11 by BirA biotin ligase; the reaction is ATP-dependent and the product biotin-lysine (biotinyl-AMP intermediate) is covalently attached.

value.mechanism: ATP-dependent enzymatic biotinylation; biotin transferred from biotin-AMP intermediate to epsilon-amine of lysine
value.specificity: single-site, sequence-specific; BirA does not biotinylate other cellular lysines under standard conditions

source.quote: In the first step, the enzyme catalyzes formation of an activated intermediate, biotinyl-59-adenylate (bio-59-AMP) from biotin and ATP. Subsequent nucleophilic attack at the activated carboxylate of biotin results in formation of the amide between the biotin moiety and the target lysine residue.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Specifies the enzymatic partner (BirA), the exact biotinylated residue (Lys11/K10), the reaction cofactor (ATP), and the intermediate species (biotin-AMP), plus specificity claim - all operationally actionable for choosing this tag and understanding how to apply it in vivo or in vitro.

source.quote: Selection of the modified peptides on this binding protein produced an extensive family of biotinylation substrate peptides that define a consensus sequence spanning a region of 13 amino acids.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Historical/bibliographic claim restating provenance. Does not discriminate AviTag from competitors, does not inform operational parameters (inducer, host, conditions, kinetics), and is generic to the part's identity. Useful primarily for citations rather than choice/operation.

functional_claims.label: AviTag enables site-specific single-biotin installation for streptavidin-based detection, affinity capture, live-cell surface protein labeling, and ChIP.

source.quote: We show that mammalian cell surface proteins tagged with AP can be biotinylated by biotin ligase added to the medium ... We targeted QDs to cell surface cyan fluorescent protein and epidermal growth factor receptor in HeLa cells and to AMPA receptors in neurons.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: The applications listed are concrete, discriminating, and operationally actionable. A designer would specifically choose AviTag over alternatives based on need for QD targeting, live-cell labeling, ChIP, or affinity capture. Each application addresses a distinct use case.

functional_claims.label: AviTag is biotinylated by E. coli BirA in vivo across a broad range of expression hosts (bacteria, insect cells, mammalian cells) and efficiently by purified BirA in vitro.

Spodoptera frugiperda insect cells (in vivo, baculovirus BirA co-expression; PMID:9750126)

value.notes: BirA must be co-expressed or added exogenously; endogenous mammalian biotin ligases do not efficiently biotinylate the peptide

source.quote: Site-specific, enzymatic biotinylation of recombinant proteins in Spodoptera frugiperda cells using biotin acceptor peptides

The claim's other hosts (mammalian, in vitro) are supported by OTHER PMIDs (15897449, 25560075) not the asserted_source. The asserted quote is the paper's title (self-referential), not a claim within the paper about broad host range.

parts.name: BC2_tag
parts.description: 12-aa linear epitope tag PDRKAAVSHWQQ (BC2T), corresponding to residues 16–27 of human beta-catenin (UniProt P35222). Bound by the BC2 nanobody (BC2-Nb) with Kd ~1.4 nM via a 'headlock' binding mode: the peptide adopts a beta-strand that integrates into a beta-sheet of the nanobody, providing affinity through backbone hydrogen bonds. Specificity is set by only four side chains (R3, A6, S8, W10, with W10 invariable); the other eight positions tolerate substitution, allowing the tag to be re-engineered. Functions as a single binding epitope with no independently used internal sub-elements.

parts.name: birA
parts.description: Bifunctional biotin-[acetyl-CoA-carboxylase] ligase / biotin-operon repressor (BirA), E. coli K12; UniProt P06709, 321 aa, ~35.3 kDa. Three-domain architecture: an N-terminal winged helix-turn-helix DNA-binding domain, a central catalytic domain (seven-stranded mixed beta-sheet bearing the active site and the biotin-binding GRGRRG loop, here at residues 115-120, 1-based), and a C-terminal antiparallel beta-sheet domain. Catalyses biotinylation in two steps: biotin + ATP -> biotinoyl-5'-AMP, then transfer of biotin to a specific lysine of the acceptor substrate. Bio-5'-AMP also acts as corepressor, driving homodimerization and sequence-specific binding to the bioO operator to repress the biotin biosynthesis operon. Parent enzyme of the AviTag/BAP site-specific biotinylation system and of the engineered proximity-labeling ligases BioID (R118G), TurboID and miniTurbo.

functional_claims.label: BirA catalyses biotinylation in two steps: it condenses biotin with ATP to form biotinoyl-5'-AMP, then transfers biotin to a specific lysine of the acceptor substrate; bio-5'-AMP is also the corepressor of the biotin operon.

value.reaction: biotin + ATP -> biotinoyl-5'-AMP (intermediate held in active site) -> biotin transferred to acceptor lysine
value.dual_function: biotin-protein ligase AND bio-5'-AMP-dependent transcriptional repressor of the biotin biosynthesis operon

source.quote: Biotinylation by BirA is a two-step process. The first of these combines biotin and ATP to form biotinoyl-5'-AMP (bioAMP). This activated biotin is held within the BirA active site until it reacts with a specific lysine residue of the BAT sequence in the second step.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Describes the core catalytic mechanism of BirA, which is essential for understanding how the part functions in biotinylation applications (AviTag, BioID, TurboID) — directly operationally relevant to anyone designing with this enzyme.
functional_claims.comment: The two-step reaction and biotinoyl-5'-AMP intermediate formation are fully supported. However, the claim's second component — that bio-5'-AMP is the corepressor of the biotin operon — is NOT addressed in the Roux 2012 paper. The paper notes BirA's repressor function but does not discuss the mechanistic role of bio-5'-AMP in repression. This claim likely draws on earlier primary literature (Barker & Campbell, or others cited in the part's references), not from Roux 2012.

functional_claims.label: BirA is a three-domain protein: an N-terminal alpha-helical helix-turn-helix DNA-binding domain, a central seven-stranded mixed beta-sheet catalytic/active-site domain, and a C-terminal antiparallel beta-sheet domain.

value.central: seven-stranded mixed beta-sheet, alpha-helices on one face, solvent-exposed face carries the active site

source.quote: The structure of BirA is highly asymmetric and consists of three domains. The N-terminal domain is mostly alpha-helical, contains a helix-turn-helix DNA-binding motif... The central domain consists of a seven-stranded mixed beta-sheet with alpha-helices covering one face. The other side of the sheet is largely solvent-exposed and contains the active site. The C-terminal domain comprises a six-stranded, antiparallel beta-sheet sandwich.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Describes operationally relevant domain architecture (DNA-binding vs catalytic domains) useful for understanding BirA variants like BioID/TurboID, but somewhat redundant with the feature note in the part description.

functional_claims.label: BirA biotinylation is highly specific in eukaryotic cells because endogenous biotinylation is restricted to a few carboxylases; this makes the BirA + acceptor-peptide system functional across mammalian and other heterologous hosts.

value.basis: background biotinylation is minimal in eukaryotes because biotin is a rare modification

source.quote: Because biotinylation is a rare modification, in mammalian cells it is restricted primarily to only a few carboxylases; BAT-independent binding is minimal.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Directly addresses a critical selection parameter for BioID/BirA deployment: explains the mechanistic basis for low background biotinylation in eukaryotes (rare natural modification, restricted to carboxylases), which is operationally actionable for anyone choosing this system for proximity labeling in non-E. coli hosts.
functional_claims.comment: The asserted host list (yeast, Drosophila, C. elegans, bacteria) is NOT validated in this paper—only mammalian cells (HEK293, HeLa) are experimentally tested. The discussion section speculates BioID should work in "essentially any accessible cell type, mammalian or otherwise" but provides no experimental evidence for non-mammalian hosts; this is theoretical extrapolation, not empirical validation.

functional_claims.label: BirA is the enzyme of the AviTag/BAP site-specific biotinylation system: co-expressed with (or applied to) a fused acceptor peptide, it biotinylates a single lysine, enabling avidin/streptavidin capture, detection, and immobilization.

value.use: site-specific in vivo or in vitro mono-biotinylation of an acceptor-peptide-tagged protein
value.cognate_substrate: AviTag / biotin acceptor peptide (BAP); 15-aa GLNDIFEAQKIEWHE derived from the 13-residue consensus

source.quote: Cell surface proteins tagged with a 15-amino acid peptide are biotinylated by Escherichia coli biotin ligase (BirA), whereas endogenous proteins are not modified. The biotin group then allows sensitive and stable binding by streptavidin conjugates. This protocol describes the optimal use of BirA and streptavidin for site-specific labeling.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Specifies the paired use (BirA + AviTag), exact substrate sequence, operational details (in vivo/in vitro), and downstream applications (capture, detection, immobilization)—directly actionable for part selection and use in biotechnology workflows.

functional_claims.label: The R118G mutant (BirA*, used in BioID) is defective in self-association and DNA binding and binds bio-5'-AMP ~100-fold more weakly than wild type, so it prematurely releases reactive bio-5'-AMP and biotinylates neighbouring proteins promiscuously within ~20-30 nm.

value.name: BirA* (BioID)
value.effect: promiscuous proximity-dependent biotinylation; ~100-fold reduced bio-5'-AMP affinity; defective self-association and DNA binding
value.position_verified: residue 118 is R in this sequence (1-based)

source.quote: One such BirA mutant (R118G, hereafter called BirA*), which is defective in both self-association and DNA binding, displays an affinity for bioAMP two orders of magnitude less than that of the wild-type enzyme... roughly 50% of detected proteins likely reside within ∼20–30 nm of the nearest LaA molecule.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Selection-relevant variant with operationally actionable parameters: specifies the ~20-30 nm proximity-labeling radius (critical design specification), quantifies bio-5'-AMP affinity loss (100-fold), and explains functional consequence (promiscuous neighbor biotinylation) that directly guides experimental use of BioID.

functional_claims.label: TurboID (35 kDa, 15 mutations vs wild-type BirA) and miniTurbo (28 kDa, N-terminal domain aa 1-63 deleted plus 13 mutations) are directed-evolution variants that catalyse proximity labeling far faster than BioID, enabling 10-minute biotin labeling in cells and organisms.

source.quote: Our engineering efforts yielded two promiscuous ligases: 35 kD TurboID, with 15 mutations relative to wild-type BirA; and 28 kD miniTurbo, with N-terminal domain deleted and 13 mutations relative to wild-type BirA... enable 10-minute PL in cells with non-toxic and easily deliverable biotin.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Provides specific, quantitative parameters (size, mutation count, deletion range, labeling time) that directly inform design choices and help users select between TurboID/miniTurbo variants for experimental applications
functional_claims.comment: Could not access full primary text to verify exact mutation counts; Addgene plasmid databases (derivative sources of the original work) consistently list 14 TurboID mutations and 12 miniTurbo mutations, not 15 and 13 as claimed.

functional_claims.label: Holo-BirA (with bound bio-5'-AMP) homodimerizes and binds the 40-bp bioO operator palindrome as a corepressor, repressing the biotin biosynthesis operon; bio-5'-AMP availability sets repressor activity.

value.role: corepressor-activated transcriptional repressor of the biotin biosynthesis (bio) operon
value.operator: bioO (~40 bp palindrome), bound cooperatively by two holo-BirA monomers
value.corepressor: biotinoyl-5'-AMP (bio-5'-AMP), which drives dimerization required for DNA binding

source.quote: Eisenstein & Beckett (1999): the adenylate-bound protein assembles into dimers with an equilibrium constant of 11 microM; site-specific DNA binding and protein dimerization are obligatorily linked in the system. Abbott & Beckett (1993): the BirA-biotin operator (bioO) complex consists of two holo-repressor monomers per operator site; the operator is a 40 base pair imperfect inverted palindrome.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Directly specifies the control logic (repressible regulation), the corepressor molecule (bio-5'-AMP) at physiologically relevant concentrations, the target operator (bioO with precise 40 bp size and binding stoichiometry), and the molecular prerequisite (homodimerization). This is essential operational knowledge for designers selecting or deploying BirA as a regulated transcriptional repressor.

parts.name: enterokinase_site
parts.description: Enterokinase (enteropeptidase) recognition/cleavage site (Asp-Asp-Asp-Asp-Lys). The protease cleaves on the C-terminal side of the Lys (after K), releasing a downstream protein with no residual vector-derived residues — hence its wide use for tag removal from fusion proteins. The acidic (Asp)4 tetrad and the P1 Lys are recognized by the enterokinase light-chain S1–S5 basic substrate pocket. Sequence DDDDK is a 100%-identity match to residues 19–23 of the trypsinogen-1 activation propeptide (UniProt:P00760), the canonical natural occurrence of the motif. This part covers a 5-aa sub-span of that 247-aa precursor (full_length_match=false), which is expected for a short recognition motif and is not a sequence defect.

parts.description: HaloTag7 self-labeling protein tag, 297 aa. Engineered monomeric haloalkane dehalogenase derived from Rhodococcus rhodochrous DhaA, carrying mutations that (i) abolish the second hydrolytic step so the enzyme-substrate covalent intermediate is trapped, and (ii) improve folding, solubility, and binding kinetics. The nucleophilic aspartate at the alpha/beta-hydrolase nucleophile elbow attacks the terminal carbon of the chloroalkane, displacing chloride; an engineered His->Phe substitution at the base catalytic histidine prevents the normal second hydrolytic step, trapping the covalent enzyme-ligand intermediate.

source.quote: The protein tag (HaloTag) is a modified haloalkane dehalogenase designed to covalently bind to synthetic ligands... Covalent bond formation between the protein tag and the chloroalkane linker is highly specific, occurs rapidly under physiological conditions, and is essentially irreversible.

functional_claims.label: This 297-aa sequence is HaloTag7, an engineered variant of Rhodococcus DhaA carrying 21 amino-acid substitutions relative to the parent dehalogenase that enhance soluble expression, folding, and ligand-binding kinetics; it is the workhorse HaloTag scaffold for downstream applications.

source.quote: we have developed a novel protein tag, HaloTag7, engineered to enhance expression and solubility of recombinant proteins and to provide efficient protein purification coupled with tag removal.

functional_claims.label: As an N-terminal fusion partner in E. coli, HaloTag7 enhanced soluble expression: 74% of 23 test human proteins were produced in soluble form when fused to HaloTag7, versus 52% for MBP, 39% for GST, and 22% for a His6 tag.

source.quote: 74% of the proteins were produced in soluble form when fused to HaloTag7 compared to 52%, 39% and 22%, respectively, for the other tags.

functional_claims.label: HaloTag7 labels its chloroalkane substrates rapidly; the apparent second-order rate constant spans more than six orders of magnitude depending on the attached fluorophore, with rhodamine-chloroalkane substrates reacting near the diffusion limit and HaloTag7 labeling more than 100-fold faster than SNAP-tag for matched substrates.

functional_claims.label: HaloTag has been deployed across bacterial, mammalian, and plant systems for labeling, imaging, and purification of fusion proteins.

parts.name: LPETG_tag
parts.description: Sortase A (SrtA) recognition motif and a single instance of the Gram-positive LPXTG cell-wall sorting signal (Leu-Pro-Glu-Thr-Gly). SrtA cleaves the scissile peptide bond between the P1 Thr and the P1' Gly (LPET|G), forming a covalent acyl-enzyme thioester intermediate at the active-site Cys; this intermediate is resolved by nucleophilic attack of an N-terminal oligoglycine (Gly-n, typically GGG/GGGGG) acceptor, ligating the upstream protein to the acceptor and regenerating an LPETGG... junction. Whole-part feature spanning all 5 residues; this is a single atomic functional element with no functional sub-features. The X position of the LPXTG consensus is most commonly Glu (E) in efficient engineering substrates. Source-verified to UniProt P02976 (Protein A) Motif 'LPXTG sorting signal' at positions 482-486, 5/5 identity.

functional_claims.label: LPETG must be placed at (or near) the C-terminus of the protein of interest and in an accessible, unstructured region; the Gly that follows it is consumed/relocated by the ligation

value.position: C-terminal of the protein of interest (commonly POI-LPETG-His6 so an affinity tag downstream is removed upon ligation)
value.structural_requirement: the LPXTG motif should lie in a flexible, solvent-accessible / unstructured region for efficient cleavage
value.junction_after_ligation: LPETGG... (the acceptor's N-terminal Gly residues become part of the product)

source.quote: The sortase-recognition sequence is usually engineered at the C terminus of the protein to be modified, with the G or A residue in amide linkage, followed by an affinity purification handle (e.g., His6) that is lost upon reaction. The efficiency of the sortase-mediated reaction depends on the flexibility and accessibility of the region comprising the sortase A-recognition motif. As long as the LPXTG/A motif is introduced in an unstructured segment of the substrate protein, sortase can recognize the sequence. An LPXTG motif that is highly structured is usually a poor sortase substrate.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Actionable constraints on C-terminal placement, accessibility requirements, linker design strategy, and junction composition are essential design parameters for successful sortagging applications

functional_claims.label: Host-agnostic sequence element: LPETG is a short peptide motif and the sortase enzyme is supplied separately, so the handle functions independently of the expression host

value.element_type: sortase recognition / cell-wall sorting signal (peptide motif)
value.host_dependence: none for the motif itself; ligation requires sortase A supplied in vitro (or co-expressed in vivo)
value.enzyme_origin: Staphylococcus aureus sortase A (SrtA); commonly the evolved pentamutant eSrtA for enhanced activity

source.quote: Sortase (SrtA), a transpeptidase from Staphylococcus aureus, catalyzes a cell-wall sorting reaction at an LPXTG motif by cleaving between threonine and glycine and subsequently joining the carboxyl group of threonine to an amino group of pentaglycine. We have applied this transpeptidyl activity of sortase to in vitro protein ligation. We found that in the presence of sortase, protein/peptide with an LPXTG motif can be specifically ligated to an aminoglycine protein/peptide via an amide bond. Additionally, sortase can even conjugate substrates such as (d)-peptides, synthetic branched peptides, and aminoglycine-derivatized small molecules to the C terminus of a recombinant protein.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Datasheet_slot claim: directly influences designer choice of expression system; explains why any organism/context works (motif is sequence + separate enzyme)

functional_claims.label: The catalyst, not the motif, is the main engineered variant: the evolved SrtA pentamutant (eSrtA) has greatly improved activity toward LPETG; orthogonal sortases instead recognize altered motifs (LAXTG / LPXSG) rather than LPETG

value.motif_variants: LPXTG class; X is most often Glu in efficient substrates (LPETG); other X residues occur in natural signals
value.evolved_catalyst: eSrtA pentamutant (P94R/D160N/D165A/K190E/K196T), reported with markedly improved activity toward LPETG
value.reported_improvement: ~140-fold increased activity vs wild-type SrtA (quantitative value from a primary text not directly accessed)
value.orthogonal_motifs: engineered sortases reprogrammed to recognize LAXTG and LPXSG, independent of canonical LPETG, enabling dual labeling

source.quote: A general strategy for the evolution of bond-forming enzymes using yeast display

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Discriminates LPETG from engineered variants, provides quantitative enzyme performance data (fold activity improvement), and specifies which evolved sortase (pentamutant) to use — directly operational for a designer choosing between WT and evolved SrtA.
functional_claims.comment: The "orthogonal_motifs" clause (LAXTG/LPXSG recognition by engineered sortases) is not discussed in the cited paper (Chen et al. 2011); it appears to conflate two separate publications. The pentamutant's actual 120-fold activity (not 140-fold) is documented in Table 1 line 791 of Chen et al.

parts.name: phiC31_attB
parts.description: phiC31 bacterial attachment site attB, the 34 bp natural target in the Streptomyces coelicolor chromosome recognized by the phiC31 large serine integrase (unidirectional; no host cofactors; cannot excise without an RDF). parts.name: SBP_tag
parts.description: Streptavidin-binding peptide (SBP-Tag), 38 aa (MDEKTTGWRGGHVVEGLAGELEQLRARLEHHPQGQREP). Synthetic peptide isolated by mRNA display that binds streptavidin with Kd ~2.5 nM; used for one-step affinity purification with mild competitive elution by biotin. parts.name: SNAP_tag
parts.description: SNAP-tag self-labeling protein domain (179 aa), engineered by directed evolution from human O6-alkylguanine-DNA alkyltransferase (hAGT/MGMT, P16455). Covalently and irreversibly transfers the benzyl group of O6-benzylguanine (BG) substrates to its active-site cysteine (the I-P-C-H-R active-site motif; reactive Cys at residue 144 in this stored numbering, equivalent to canonical hAGT Cys145), thereby self-labeling the fusion protein with whatever chemical probe is appended to the BG. The 178-aa core (residues 2-179) is a 100% exact match to UniProt E5BBQ0 (182 aa, derived from PDB 3KZY) and PDB 3KZY chain A. The functional M-start ORF (179 aa) is used here; the deposited crystallography construct (E5BBQ0/3KZY, 182 aa) instead carries a GPGS cloning prefix in place of the initiator Met. Closest canonical reference: UniProt E5BBQ0 (unreviewed TrEMBL).

source.quote: By engineering the adhesin RrgA from Streptococcus pneumoniae, we developed the peptide SnoopTag, which formed a spontaneous isopeptide bond to its protein partner SnoopCatcher with >99% yield and no cross-reaction to SpyTag/SpyCatcher. A spontaneous isopeptide bond forms in the D4 Ig-like domain of RrgA between residues Lys742 and Asn854. We split the D4 domain in the loop following the N-terminal β-strand and, after exploring various extensions and truncations of the two partners, settled on the peptide tag we termed SnoopTag (residues 734–745) and the protein partner we named SnoopCatcher (residues 749–860). Mutations in the putative reactive Lys742 of SnoopTag (SnoopTag KA-MBP) and the putative reactive Asn854 of SnoopCatcher (SnoopCatcher NA) abolished reaction.

functional_claims.usefulness_rationale: Describes the core functional mechanism (isopeptide bond between Lys and Asn) and partner (SnoopCatcher), directly actionable for designers choosing and deploying this part; specifies reaction chemistry and irreversibility, differentiates from SpyTag.

parts.name: SpyCatcher
parts.description: 116-aa protein partner of the SpyTag/SpyCatcher covalent-conjugation system, engineered by splitting the CnaB2 immunoglobulin-like collagen-adhesion domain of Streptococcus pyogenes fibronectin-binding protein FbaB into a protein fragment (SpyCatcher, contains the reactive Lys31) and a 13-aa peptide (SpyTag, contains the reactive Asp). On mixing with SpyTag, the side-chain amine of SpyCatcher Lys31 spontaneously and irreversibly forms an intermolecular isopeptide (amide) bond to SpyTag Asp7, catalysed by SpyCatcher Glu77; the linkage resists boiling in SDS and forces up to ~1 nN. As stored the domain begins with a vector-derived Gly-Ala-Met (residues 24-139 of AFD50637.1, the standard post-TEV-cleavage product) and is fusable at a protein N- or C-terminus. Structural and biochemical work shows the N-terminal ~23 and C-terminal ~9 residues are dispensable, defining a minimized 84-aa core (original residues 21-104).

parts.name: SpyTag002
parts.description: Second-generation 14-aa peptide tag (VPTIVMVDAYKRYK) derived from the CnaB2 immunoglobulin-like domain of Streptococcus pyogenes fibronectin-binding protein FbaB, evolved from the original SpyTag (AHIVMVDAYKPTK) by phage-display selection plus rational design. SpyTag002 differs from SpyTag at 4/13 shared residues and carries one extra N-terminal residue. On mixing with its protein partner SpyCatcher002 it forms a spontaneous, irreversible intermolecular isopeptide (amide) bond (reactive Asp; partner Lys; catalysed by SpyCatcher Glu77); single Asp->Ala (DA) mutation abolishes the reaction. Fusable at the N-terminus or C-terminus. Backwards-compatible with the parental SpyCatcher.

parts.name: SpyTag003
parts.description: 16-aa third-generation Spy peptide tag (RGVPHIVMVDAYKRYK). Engineered from SpyTag002 by appending an N-terminal Arg-Gly (RG) dipeptide and introducing the T112H mutation (CnaB2 numbering), descended from the CnaB2 domain of Streptococcus pyogenes FbaB. On mixing with its 116-aa partner SpyCatcher003 it forms a spontaneous, irreversible intermolecular isopeptide (amide) bond between its reactive Asp (CnaB2 Asp117) and Lys31 of SpyCatcher003, catalysed by SpyCatcher Glu77 - reaction approaches the diffusion limit. Fusable at N-terminus, C-terminus or internally. Contains no Cys.
